title: Ta'ameya
subtitle: Egyptian falafel made from dried fava beans
excerpt: Egyptian falafel made from dried split fava beans instead of chickpeas — greener, softer inside, and more herbaceous than the Israeli version. Ground with fresh coriander, parsley, garlic, and cumin, shaped into patties, rolled in sesame seeds, and fried until crisp outside and vivid green within.
sourceNotes: Ta'ameya is the Egyptian falafel, claimed by Egypt as a national dish. Unlike Levantine falafel made from chickpeas, the Egyptian version uses dried split fava beans, which produce a more tender, intensely green interior. The sesame coating is characteristic. It is street food, sold from early morning in Egyptian cities, particularly Alexandria and Cairo.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze raw, shaped. Fry from frozen, adding 2 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Raw mixture keeps refrigerated for 1 day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dried beans must soak overnight — plan ahead.

content.text: The fava beans must be soaked overnight and used raw — not cooked. This is the critical point. Cooking the beans before grinding produces a soft, wet mixture that falls apart in the oil. The raw soaked bean has the right starch structure to bind and fry cleanly. Soak for at least 8 hours, drain well, and process without cooking.

The mixture should be processed to a coarse, slightly grainy paste — not smooth. If it is too fine, the ta'ameya will be dense. Process in short pulses. The mixture should hold together when pressed but still feel slightly textured. Refrigerate for 30 minutes after mixing to firm it up before shaping.

items.prepNote: soaked overnight in cold water, drained

content.text: Drain the soaked {{dried-split-fava-beans}} thoroughly. Combine with the {{coriander}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{garlic}}, {{onion}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a food processor. Pulse until a coarse paste forms — do not over-process. Refrigerate for 30 minutes. Shape the mixture into small flat patties about 4 cm across and 1.5 cm thick. Press each patty into {{sesame-seeds}} to coat both sides. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep pan to 180°C. Fry in batches for 3 to 4 minutes until deep golden brown and crisp. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve with tahini sauce and pitta or flatbread.

items.symptom: Ta'ameya falling apart in the oil
items.cause: Beans not soaked long enough, mixture too wet, or over-processed
items.fix: Drain beans very thoroughly after soaking. The mixture should be dry enough to hold a shape when pressed. Add a tablespoon of plain flour to bind if needed. Refrigerate before frying

items.cause: Too much coriander stem or over-processed
items.fix: The vivid green colour comes from the fresh herb chlorophyll. Under-processing is better than over — stop pulsing when the mixture is coarse but uniform

items.cause: Beans cooked before processing or mixture too fine
items.fix: Use raw soaked beans only. The coarser the texture of the processed mixture, the lighter the ta'ameya will be after frying

content.text: Ta'ameya is sold from early morning in Egyptian cities, served in pitta or on a plate with tahini, salad, and pickles. Cairo's breakfast culture revolves around ta'ameya and ful medames — both fava bean dishes eaten before the working day. The green interior and the sesame crust are the identifying marks. Egyptians are proprietary about ta'ameya: it is distinct from Levantine chickpea falafel, which they regard as an inferior relative. Outside Egypt it remains less known than its chickpea equivalent but rewards anyone who seeks it out.
